questions.topic: Attribute types / boxplot interpretation of standardized attributes
questions.question_text:
We will consider a basketball dataset containing 54 National Basketball Association (NBA) basketball players and their performance. For brevity this dataset will be denoted the Basketball dataset in the following. In Table 1 the attributes of the data as well as the output attribute y defined by each player's average points scored per game are given. The attributes are: x1 = Height (in feet) (Height), x2 = Weight (in pounds) (Weight), x3 = Percent of successful field goals out of 100 attempted (FG), x4 = Percent of successful free throws out of 100 attempted (FT), and the output y = average points scored per game (PT). In Figure 1 is given a boxplot of the four attributes x1-x4 after standardizing the data, i.e. subtracting the mean of each attribute and dividing each attribute by its standard deviation. The worst performing player (i.e., the player with lowest value of y) is indicated by a black circle with an asteric inside (his standardized values are Height = 0.68, Weight = 0.66, FG = -0.67, FT = -1.47). Considering the attributes described in Table 1 and the boxplot in Figure 1 which one of the following statements regarding the attributes x1-x4 and output variable y is correct?

A. The player with worst performance has Weight (i.e., x2) that is between the 25th and 50th percentile.
B. The player with worst performance has FT (i.e., x4) so low it is deemed an outlier.
C. The input attributes FT and FG (i.e., x3 and x4) are both ordinal variables.
D. The output y is ratio.

options.A: The player with worst performance has Weight (i.e., x2) that is between the 25th and 50th percentile.
options.B: The player with worst performance has FT (i.e., x4) so low it is deemed an outlier.
options.C: The input attributes FT and FG (i.e., x3 and x4) are both ordinal variables.

questions.source_location: exam p.2, Figure 1 (boxplot of four standardized attributes) + Table 1 (attribute descriptions)

questions.topic: PCA / fourth singular value from squared Frobenius norm
questions.question_text:
A principal component analysis (PCA) is carried out on the standardized attributes x1-x4, forming the standardized matrix X_tilde. The squared Frobenius norm of the standardized matrix is given by ||X_tilde||_F^2 = 212. A singular value decomposition is applied to the matrix X_tilde and we find that the first three singular values are sigma1 = 11.1, sigma2 = 7.2, sigma3 = 5.2. What is the value of the fourth singular value sigma4?

A. sigma4 = 1.2
B. sigma4 = 2.3
C. sigma4 = 3.1
D. sigma4 = 9.9

options.A: sigma4 = 1.2
options.B: sigma4 = 2.3
options.C: sigma4 = 3.1

questions.source_location: exam p.3, Question 2 (squared Frobenius norm and singular values inline)

questions.topic: PCA / identifying a projected observation in the PCA plane
questions.question_text:
From the singular value decomposition of X_tilde we further obtain the following V matrix:

V =
[ -0.60   0.02  -0.41   0.69 ]
[ -0.61   0     -0.33  -0.72 ]
[ -0.46   0.46   0.76   0.04 ]
[  0.25   0.89  -0.39  -0.04 ]

The data projected onto the first two principal components is given in Figure 2 including four observations denoted A, B, C, and D that are marked by a black circle with an asteric inside. One of these four observations corresponds to the worst performing player indicated by a similar black circle with an asteric inside in the boxplot of Figure 1 (the worst performing player has standardized observation vector x_tilde* = [0.68, 0.66, -0.67, -1.47]). Which one of the four observations in Figure 2 corresponds to the worst performing player indicated in the boxplots of Figure 1?

A. Observation A.
B. Observation B.
C. Observation C.
D. Observation D.

options.A: Observation A.
options.B: Observation B.
options.C: Observation C.

questions.source_location: exam p.3, Figure 2 (PCA projection scatter with observations A, B, C, D marked) + V matrix inline

questions.topic: Forward/backward feature selection from RMSE table
questions.question_text:
A least squares linear regression model is trained using different combinations of the four attributes x1, x2, x3, and x4 in order to predict the average points scored per game y. Table 2 provides the training and test root-mean-square error (RMSE = sqrt((1/N) sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - y_hat_i)^2)) performance of the least squares linear regression model when trained using different combinations of the four attributes. Which one of the following statements is correct?

Table 2 (Training RMSE, Test RMSE per feature subset):
Feature(s)                          Training   Test
No features                         5.8977     5.8505
x1                                  5.8760     6.0035
x2                                  5.8841     5.9037
x3                                  5.1832     5.9272
x4                                  5.8727     5.6845
x1 and x2                           5.6272     7.4558
x1 and x3                           5.1482     5.6409
x1 and x4                           5.8451     5.8269
x2 and x3                           5.0483     5.6656
x2 and x4                           5.8660     5.7461
x3 and x4                           5.1125     5.7390
x1 and x2 and x3                    4.9836     6.2823
x1 and x2 and x4                    5.6261     7.3888
x1 and x3 and x4                    5.0839     5.5099
x2 and x3 and x4                    5.0113     5.5605
x1 and x2 and x3 and x4             4.9645     6.0892

A. Forward selection will terminate when all features x1-x4 are included in the feature set.
B. The solution identified by Forward selection will be worse than the solution identified by Backward selection.
C. Forward selection will terminate using two feature in the feature set.
D. Backward selection will terminate using two features in the feature set.

options.A: Forward selection will terminate when all features x1-x4 are included in the feature set.
options.B: The solution identified by Forward selection will be worse than the solution identified by Backward selection.
options.C: Forward selection will terminate using two feature in the feature set.
options.D: Backward selection will terminate using two features in the feature set.

questions.text_representation:
Table 2 (Root-mean-square error for the training and test set using least squares regression to predict average points scored per game y, for different combinations of the four attributes x1-x4):
Feature(s)                          Training_RMSE   Test_RMSE
No features                         5.8977          5.8505
x1                                  5.8760          6.0035
x2                                  5.8841          5.9037
x3                                  5.1832          5.9272
x4                                  5.8727          5.6845
x1 and x2                           5.6272          7.4558
x1 and x3                           5.1482          5.6409
x1 and x4                           5.8451          5.8269
x2 and x3                           5.0483          5.6656
x2 and x4                           5.8660          5.7461
x3 and x4                           5.1125          5.7390
x1 and x2 and x3                    4.9836          6.2823
x1 and x2 and x4                    5.6261          7.3888
x1 and x3 and x4                    5.0839          5.5099
x2 and x3 and x4                    5.0113          5.5605
x1 and x2 and x3 and x4             4.9645          6.0892
questions.source_location: exam p.5, Table 2 (training/test RMSE for all feature subsets)

questions.topic: Gaussian distributions / matching covariance matrices to contour plot
questions.question_text:
We will encode the output attribute y in terms of three different classes, i.e. low performing players having performance below the 33.3 percentile, mid-performing players having performance in the range of the 33.3 percentile to 66.6 percentile and high-performing players having performance above the 66.6 percentile (i.e, each of the three classes will contain approximately one third of the data). We presently consider only the attributes FG and FT. Consider the three Gaussian distributions given in Figure 3 where each Gaussian is fitted to each of the three classes separately. We recall that the multivariate Gaussian distribution is given by: N(x | mu, Sigma) = (1/((2pi)^(M/2) |Sigma|^(1/2))) exp(-(1/2)(x - mu)^T Sigma^(-1) (x - mu)), with mean mu and covariance matrix Sigma. The three fitted covariances (in arbitrary order) are given by
Sigma_a = [[0.0035, 0.0003],[0.0003, 0.0030]],
Sigma_b = [[0.0028, -0.0013],[-0.0013, 0.0191]],
and Sigma_c = [[0.0020, 0.0001],[0.0001, 0.0061]].
The Gaussians are plotted in Figure 3 in terms of lines indicating where N(x | mu, Sigma) = 5, N(x | mu, Sigma) = 10, and N(x | mu, Sigma) = 20 thus defining ellipsoidal shapes at three different levels of the density functions. Which of the three classes correspond to which of the three covariance matrices Sigma_a, Sigma_b, and Sigma_c?

A. Sigma_a corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_c corresponds to the high performing class.
B. Sigma_a corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_c corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the high performing class.
C. Sigma_c corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_a corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the high performing class.
D. Sigma_c corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_a corresponds to the high performing class.

options.A: Sigma_a corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_c corresponds to the high performing class.
options.B: Sigma_a corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_c corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the high performing class.
options.C: Sigma_c corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_a corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the high performing class.
options.D: Sigma_c corresponds to the low performing class, Sigma_b corresponds to the mid performing class, Sigma_a corresponds to the high performing class.

questions.source_location: exam p.4, Figure 3 (contour plot of three fitted Gaussians in FG-FT plane) + covariance matrices inline

questions.topic: Decision tree / purity gain with Gini impurity
questions.question_text:
A decision tree is fitted to the data considering as output whether the basketball player was in the group performing low, mid, or high according to splitting the output value in terms of the 33.3 and 66.6 percentile. At the root of the tree it is considered to split according to Height (i.e., x1), considering relatively short, medium, and tall players based on splitting x1 also according to its 33.3 and 66.6 percentiles. For impurity we will use the Gini given by I(v) = 1 - sum_c p(c|v)^2. Before the split, we have 18 low, 18 mid, and 18 high performing players and after the split we have
- Of the 18 short players we have that 6 have low, 9 have mid, and 3 have high performance.
- Of the 20 medium height players we have that 4 have low, 6 have mid, and 10 have high performance.
- Of the 16 tall players we have that 8 have low, 3 have mid, and 5 have high performance.
Which statement regarding the purity gain Delta of the split is correct?

A. Delta = 0.0505
B. Delta = 0.1667
C. Delta = 0.3333
D. Delta = 0.6667

options.A: Delta = 0.0505
options.B: Delta = 0.1667
options.C: Delta = 0.3333

questions.source_location: exam p.6, Question 6 (class counts before/after split given inline)

questions.topic: ROC curve / matching AUC for a feature discriminator
questions.question_text:
We suspect that a basketball player's Height (i.e., x1) is predictive of whether the player is successful with his field goals FG (i.e., x3). To quantify whether Height is predictive of FG we will evaluate the area under curve (AUC) of the receiver operator characteristic (ROC) using the feature Height to discriminate between FG > 45% (positive class) or FG <= 45% (negative class) considering the data given in Figure 4. Figure 4 shows the attribute Height for 10 observations O1,...,O10 placed along a 1D Height axis, where O5, O6, O7, and O10 have FG > 45% (positive class, black) and O1, O2, O3, O4, O8, and O9 have FG <= 45% (negative class, red). Figure 5 shows four different ROC curves (TPR vs FPR) with AUC values 0.625, 0.750, 0.875, and 0.917. Which one of the receiver operator characteristic (ROC) curves given in Figure 5 corresponds to the correct ROC curve?

A. The curve having AUC=0.625
B. The curve having AUC=0.750
C. The curve having AUC=0.875
D. The curve having AUC=0.917

options.A: The curve having AUC=0.625
options.B: The curve having AUC=0.750
options.C: The curve having AUC=0.875

questions.source_location: exam p.7, Figure 5 (four ROC curves with AUC values) + Figure 4 (Height number-line with positive/negative class)

questions.topic: k-means clustering on 1D Height values (converged solution)
questions.question_text:
We will consider the ten observations of the Basketball dataset given in Figure 4. We will cluster this data using k-means with Euclidean distance into two clusters (i.e., k=2). Which one of the following solutions constitutes a converged solution in the k-means clustering procedure?

Figure 4 (value of attribute Height for the ten observations O1-O10, read along the Height axis):
Observation : Height
O1 : 5.7
O2 : 6.0
O3 : 6.2
O4 : 6.3
O5 : 6.4
O6 : 6.6
O7 : 6.7
O8 : 6.9
O9 : 7.0
O10 : 7.4
(O5, O6, O7, O10 have FG > 45% (black); O1, O2, O3, O4, O8, O9 have FG <= 45% (red).)

A. {O1}, {O2, O3, O4, O5, O6, O7, O8, O9, O10}.
B. {O1, O2, O3, O4, O5}, {O6, O7, O8, O9, O10}.
C. {O1, O2, O3, O4, O5, O6, O7}, {O8, O9, O10}.
D. {O1, O2, O3, O4, O5, O6, O7, O8, O9}, {O10}.
questions.text_representation:
Figure 4 (value of attribute Height for the ten observations O1-O10, read along the Height axis spanning 5.7 to 7.4):
Observation : Height
O1 : 5.7
O2 : 6.0
O3 : 6.2
O4 : 6.3
O5 : 6.4
O6 : 6.6
O7 : 6.7
O8 : 6.9
O9 : 7.0
O10 : 7.4
Class colors: FG > 45% (black) = {O5, O6, O7, O10}; FG <= 45% (red) = {O1, O2, O3, O4, O8, O9}.
questions.source_location: exam p.5/8, Figure 4 (1D Height number-line for O1-O10 with values readable on axis)

questions.topic: Average relative KNN density / outlier detection on 1D Height values
questions.question_text:
We suspect that observation O10 may be an outlier. In order to assess if this is the case we would like to calculate the average relative KNN density based on Euclidean distance and the observations given in Figure 4 only. We recall that the KNN density and average relative density (ard) for the observation x_i are given by:
density_{X\i}(x_i, K) = 1 / ( (1/K) sum_{x' in N_{X\i}(x_i,K)} d(x_i, x') ),
ard_X(x_i, K) = density_{X\i}(x_i, K) / ( (1/K) sum_{x_j in N_{X\i}(x_i,K)} density_{X\j}(x_j, K) ),
where N_{X\i}(x_i, K) is the set of K nearest neighbors of observation x_i excluding the i'th observation, and ard_X(x_i, K) is the average relative density of x_i using K nearest neighbors (if observations are tied in terms of their distances to an observation, the observation with smallest observation number will be selected). Based on considering only the attribute Height and the ten observations in Figure 4, what is the average relative density for observation O10 for K = 3 nearest neighbors?

Figure 4 (value of attribute Height for the ten observations O1-O10, read along the Height axis):
Observation : Height
O1 : 5.7
O2 : 6.0
O3 : 6.2
O4 : 6.3
O5 : 6.4
O6 : 6.6
O7 : 6.7
O8 : 6.9
O9 : 7.0
O10 : 7.4

A. 0.409
B. 0.500
C. 0.533
D. 1.875
questions.text_representation:
Figure 4 (value of attribute Height for the ten observations O1-O10, read along the Height axis spanning 5.7 to 7.4):
Observation : Height
O1 : 5.7
O2 : 6.0
O3 : 6.2
O4 : 6.3
O5 : 6.4
O6 : 6.6
O7 : 6.7
O8 : 6.9
O9 : 7.0
O10 : 7.4
(The three nearest neighbors of O10 are O7, O8, O9 at distances 0.7, 0.5, 0.4 respectively.)
questions.source_location: exam p.8, Figure 4 (1D Height number-line for O1-O10 with values readable on axis)

questions.topic: Hierarchical clustering / matching dendrogram (average linkage)
questions.question_text:
We will consider the Euclidean distance between the observations in Figure 4 based on the attribute Height only (i.e., the Euclidean distance between observation O1 and O2 is sqrt((5.7 - 6.0)^2) = 0.3). A hierarchical clustering is used to cluster the observations based on their distances to each other using average linkage (when ties in the agglomerative procedure clusters containing the smallest observation numbers will merge first). Which one of the dendrograms given in Figure 6 corresponds to the clustering?

Figure 6 shows four candidate dendrograms (Dendrogram 1, 2, 3, 4) derived using the Euclidean distance between the 10 observations based on the Height attribute.

A. Dendrogram 1.
B. Dendrogram 2.
C. Dendrogram 3.
D. Dendrogram 4.

options.A: Dendrogram 1.
options.B: Dendrogram 2.
options.C: Dendrogram 3.

questions.source_location: exam p.9, Figure 6 (four candidate dendrograms) + Figure 4 Height values

questions.topic: Rand index (SMC) between dendrogram-2 cut and class labels
questions.question_text:
We will cut dendrogram 2 at the level of two clusters and evaluate this clustering in terms of its correspondence with the class label information in which O1, O2, O3, O4, O8, and O9 correspond to low values of FG whereas O5, O6, O7, and O10 correspond to high values of FG. We recall that the Rand index also denoted the simple matching coefficient (SMC) between the true labels and the extracted clusters is given by R = (f11 + f00) / K, where f11 is the number of object pairs in same class assigned to same cluster, f00 is the number of object pairs in different class assigned to different clusters, and K = N(N - 1)/2 is the total number of object pairs, where N is the number of observations considered. What is the value of R between the true labeling of the observations in terms of high and low FG values and the two clusters?

A. 0.3226
B. 0.5333
C. 0.5778
D. 0.6222
questions.source_location: exam p.10, Question 11 (requires reading the two-cluster cut of Dendrogram 2 in Figure 6); class labels given in text

questions.topic: Gaussian Mixture Model / posterior cluster assignment probability (1D)
questions.question_text:
We will fit a Gaussian mixture model (GMM) with three clusters to the 10 observations given in Figure 4. The fitted density is given in Figure 7 in which the overall density is given in gray and the contribution of each Gaussian given in blue. We recall that the Gaussian mixture model for 1-dimensional data is given by: p(x) = sum_k w_k (1/sqrt(2 pi sigma_k^2)) exp(-(1/(2 sigma_k^2))(x - mu_k)^2). For the clustering we have:
w1 = 0.37, w2 = 0.29, w3 = 0.34,
mu1 = 6.12, mu2 = 6.55, mu3 = 6.93,
sigma1^2 = 0.09, sigma2^2 = 0.13, sigma3^2 = 0.12.
The Height of observation O8 (read from Figure 4) is x8 = 6.9. What is the probability that observation O8 is assigned to cluster 2 according to the GMM?

A. 0.20
B. 0.29
C. 0.33
D. 0.37
questions.text_representation:
GMM parameters (three clusters, 1-dimensional data on attribute Height):
w1 = 0.37, w2 = 0.29, w3 = 0.34
mu1 = 6.12, mu2 = 6.55, mu3 = 6.93
sigma1^2 = 0.09, sigma2^2 = 0.13, sigma3^2 = 0.12
Height of observation O8 (from Figure 4): x8 = 6.9.
questions.source_location: exam p.10, Question 12 (GMM parameters inline; Figure 7 GMM density and Figure 4 give O8 Height = 6.9)

questions.topic: Confusion matrix / recall, precision, accuracy, class balance
questions.question_text:
We will consider a simple classifier that predicts the 54 basketball players as having high success rate of field goals (FG > 45%, considered the positive class) if they are taller than 6.65 foot and low otherwise (FG <= 45%, considered the negative class). The confusion matrix of the classifier is given in Figure 8. Which statement regarding the classifier is correct?

Figure 8 (confusion matrix; rows = Actual class, columns = Predicted class):
                                Predicted Positive (FG>45%)   Predicted Negative (FG<=45%)
Actual Positive (FG>45%)                 18                            12
Actual Negative (FG<=45%)                 9                            15

A. The recall of the classifier is 60.0 %.
B. The precision of the classifier is 61.1 %.
C. The accuracy of the classifier is 66.7 %.
D. The dataset is perfectly balanced.

options.A: The recall of the classifier is 60.0 %.
options.B: The precision of the classifier is 61.1 %.
options.C: The accuracy of the classifier is 66.7 %.

questions.text_representation:
Figure 8 (confusion matrix of the classifier; rows = Actual class, columns = Predicted class):
                                Predicted Positive (FG>45%)   Predicted Negative (FG<=45%)
Actual Positive (FG>45%)                 18                            12
Actual Negative (FG<=45%)                 9                            15
(TP=18, FN=12, FP=9, TN=15; 30 positive and 24 negative examples.)
questions.source_location: exam p.11, Figure 8 (2x2 confusion matrix with counts 18/12/9/15)

questions.topic: Bayes theorem from conditional probabilities
questions.question_text:
The National Basketball Association (NBA) is the top basketball league in USA and all males playing in the NBA earns more than several million dollars a year or more making them all have a very high salary. In USA we will assume approximately 0.2 % of the male population that are not playing in the NBA makes such similar very high salary. Furthermore, we will assume two out of a million American males are playing in the NBA. Assuming the above, what is the probability that a male in USA making such very high salary plays in the NBA?

A. 0.0002%
B. 0.0010%
C. 0.0999%
D. 0.2002%
questions.source_location: exam p.11, Question 14 (probabilities given inline)

questions.topic: Classifier identification from decision boundaries
questions.question_text:
Four different classifiers are trained on the Basketball dataset considering the features Height and Weight in order to predict if the percentage of successful field goals is high (FG > 45%) or low (FG <= 45%). The decision boundary for each of the four classifiers is given in Figure 9 (gray regions classify into red crosses whereas white regions into black plusses). Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. Classifier 1 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2, Classifier 2 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance, Classifier 3 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 4 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2.
B. Classifier 1 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance, Classifier 2 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 3 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2, Classifier 4 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2.
C. Classifier 1 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2, Classifier 2 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance, Classifier 3 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 4 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2.
D. Classifier 1 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 2 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2, Classifier 3 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2, Classifier 4 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance.

options.A: Classifier 1 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2, Classifier 2 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance, Classifier 3 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 4 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2.
options.B: Classifier 1 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance, Classifier 2 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 3 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2, Classifier 4 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2.
options.C: Classifier 1 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2, Classifier 2 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance, Classifier 3 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 4 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2.
options.D: Classifier 1 is a decision tree including three decisions, Classifier 2 corresponds to logistic regression considering as input x1, x2, and x1*x2, Classifier 3 corresponds to a logistic regression considering as input x1 and x2, Classifier 4 is a 3-nearest neighbor classifier using Euclidean distance.

questions.source_location: exam p.12, Figure 9 (four decision-boundary plots in Height-Weight plane)

questions.topic: Artificial neural network / forward pass with ReLU activation
questions.question_text:
We will consider an artificial neural network (ANN) trained to predict the average score of a player (i.e., y). The ANN is based on the model: f(x, w) = w_0^(2) + sum_{j=1}^{2} w_j^(2) h^(1)([1 x] w_j^(1)), where h^(1)(x) = max(x, 0) is the rectified linear function used as activation function in the hidden layer (i.e., positive values are returned and negative values are set to zero). We will consider an ANN with two hidden units in the hidden layer defined by:
w_1^(1) = [21.78, -1.65, 0, -13.26, -8.46]^T, w_2^(1) = [-9.60, -0.44, 0.01, 14.54, 9.50]^T,
and w_0^(2) = 2.84, w_1^(2) = 3.25, and w_2^(2) = 3.46.
What is the predicted average score of a basketball player with observation vector x* = [6.8, 225, 0.44, 0.68]?

A. 1.00
B. 3.74
C. 8.21
D. 11.54
questions.source_location: exam p.13, Question 16 (ANN weights and observation vector inline)

questions.topic: Cross-validation concepts
questions.question_text:
Which statement regarding cross-validation is correct?

A. An advantage of five-fold cross-validation over three-fold cross-validation is that the datasets used for training are larger.
B. The more data used for training a model the more we can expect the model to overfit to the training data.
C. 10-fold cross-validation is more accurate but also more computationally expensive than leave-one-out cross-validation.
D. When upsampling data in order to avoid class imbalance issues the same observations should be included in the training and test set such that the training and test sets reflect the same properties.

options.A: An advantage of five-fold cross-validation over three-fold cross-validation is that the datasets used for training are larger.
options.B: The more data used for training a model the more we can expect the model to overfit to the training data.
options.C: 10-fold cross-validation is more accurate but also more computationally expensive than leave-one-out cross-validation.
options.D: When upsampling data in order to avoid class imbalance issues the same observations should be included in the training and test set such that the training and test sets reflect the same properties.

questions.source_location: exam p.13, Question 17 (all text)

questions.topic: Frequent itemsets / support threshold from binarized market-basket matrix
questions.question_text:
Considering the dataset in Table 3 as a market basket problem with observation O1-O10 corresponding to customers and HL, HH, WL, WH, FG<=45%, FG>45%, FT<=75%, and FT>75% corresponding to items. What are all frequent itemsets with support greater than 35%?

Table 3 (ten observations binarized; columns HL, HH, WL, WH, FG<=45%, FG>45%, FT<=75%, FT>75%):
      HL  HH  WL  WH  FG<=45%  FG>45%  FT<=75%  FT>75%
O1     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O2     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O3     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O4     1   0   1   0     1        0       0        1
O5     1   0   1   0     0        1       0        1
O6     1   0   0   1     0        1       1        0
O7     0   1   1   0     0        1       0        1

questions.topic: Association rule confidence from binarized market-basket matrix
questions.question_text:
We consider again the data in Table 3 as a market basket problem. What is the confidence of the association rule {HL, WL} -> {FG<=45%, FT<=75%}?

questions.topic: Naive Bayes classifier from binarized matrix
questions.question_text:
We would like to predict whether a basketball player has a high average score using the data in Table 3. We will apply a Naive Bayes classifier that assumes independence between the attributes given the class label (i.e., the class label is given by the average points scored per game being low (black color), in the mid-range (red color) or high (blue color) respectively in the table). Given that a basketball player is relatively tall (HH = 1) relatively light weight (WL = 1) what is the probability that the basketball player will have a high average score according to the Naive Bayes classifier derived from the data in Table 3?

Table 3 (ten observations binarized; columns HL, HH, WL, WH, FG<=45%, FG>45%, FT<=75%, FT>75%; class by average points scored per game y: low {O2,O3,O6,O9}, mid {O1,O4}, high {O5,O7,O8,O10}):
      HL  HH  WL  WH  FG<=45%  FG>45%  FT<=75%  FT>75%
O1     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O2     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O3     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O4     1   0   1   0     1        0       0        1
O5     1   0   1   0     0        1       0        1
O6     1   0   0   1     0        1       1        0
O7     0   1   1   0     0        1       0        1
O8     0   1   1   0     1        0       0        1
O9     0   1   0   1     1        0       1        0
O10    0   1   0   1     0        1       1        0

A. 9/16
B. 9/11
C. 3/4
D. 1

questions.topic: 3-nearest neighbor classifier with Jaccard similarity from binary matrix
questions.question_text:
Considering the data in Table 3, we will use a 3-nearest neighbor classifier to classify observation O10 (i.e., with binary observation vector [0 1 0 1 0 1 1 0]) based on observation O1-O9. We will classify according to the three neighboring observations with highest similarity according to the Jaccard (J) measure of similarity given by J(a,b) = f11 / (M - f00), where f11 and f00 are the number of one matches and zero matches respectively and M the total number of binary features. Which one of the following statements is correct?

Table 3 (ten observations binarized; columns HL, HH, WL, WH, FG<=45%, FG>45%, FT<=75%, FT>75%; class colors: black {O2,O3,O6,O9}, red {O1,O4}, blue {O5,O7,O8,O10}):
      HL  HH  WL  WH  FG<=45%  FG>45%  FT<=75%  FT>75%
O1     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O2     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O3     1   0   1   0     1        0       1        0
O4     1   0   1   0     1        0       0        1
O5     1   0   1   0     0        1       0        1
O6     1   0   0   1     0        1       1        0
O7     0   1   1   0     0        1       0        1
O8     0   1   1   0     1        0       0        1
O9     0   1   0   1     1        0       1        0
O10    0   1   0   1     0        1       1        0

A. O10 will be classified as black.
B. O10 will be classified as blue.
C. O10 will be classified as red.
D. The classifier will be tied between the classes black and blue.

options.A: O10 will be classified as black.
options.B: O10 will be classified as blue.
options.C: O10 will be classified as red.
options.D: The classifier will be tied between the classes black and blue.

questions.topic: AdaBoost / classification after two boosting rounds from decision boundaries
questions.question_text:
We will consider classifying the 10 observations considered in Figure 4 using logistic regression and boosting by Adaboost (notice, the Adaboost algorithm uses the natural logarithm). For this purpose we include only two boosting rounds considering only the features Height (i.e., x1) and Weight (i.e., x2) as inputs. In the first round the data is sampled with equal probability w_i = 1/10 for i = {1,...,10} and the logistic regression model with decision boundary given to the left of Figure 10 trained. A new dataset is subsequently sampled and a new logistic regression classifier given to the right of the Figure 10 trained. Figure 10 shows the decision boundaries for the two rounds (Round 1 and Round 2) in the Height-Weight plane (gray region classifies as red cross, white region as black plus). Based on these two rounds of the Adaboost algorithm what will an observation located at x1 = 6 and x2 = 240 be classified as?

A. The two classes will be tied for the Adaboost procedure.
B. The observation will be classified as red cross.
C. The observation will be classified as black plus.
D. The weights w1,...,w10 are changed in round 1 of the Adaboost procedure.

options.A: The two classes will be tied for the Adaboost procedure.
options.B: The observation will be classified as red cross.
options.C: The observation will be classified as black plus.
options.D: The weights w1,...,w10 are changed in round 1 of the Adaboost procedure.

questions.source_location: exam p.16, Figure 10 (decision boundaries for two AdaBoost rounds in Height-Weight plane)

questions.topic: Regularized (ridge) regression / interpreting training vs test RMSE curves
questions.question_text:
Using the 54 observations of the Basketball dataset we would like to predict the average points scored per game (y) based on the four features (x1-x4). For this purpose we consider regularized least squares regression which minimizes with respect to w the following cost function: E(w) = sum_n (y_n - [1 x_n1 x_n2 x_n3 x_n4] w)^2 + lambda w^T w, where x_nm denotes the m'th feature of the n'th observation, and 1 is concatenated to the data to account for the bias term. We consider 20 different values of lambda and use leave-one-out cross-validation to quantify the performance of each of these different values of lambda. The results of the leave-one-out cross-validation performance is given in Figure 11. Figure 11 plots two RMSE curves (a blue curve with circles and a black curve with crosses) as a function of log10(lambda) (x-axis from -3 to 3). Inspecting the model for the value of lambda = 0.6952 the following model is identified: f(x) = 2.76 - 0.37*x1 + 0.01*x2 + 7.67*x3 + 7.67*x4. Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. In Figure 11 the blue curve with circles corresponds to the training error whereas the black curve with crosses corresponds to the test error.
B. According to the model defined for lambda = 0.6952 increasing a players height will increase his average points scored per game.
C. There is no optimal way of choosing lambda since increasing lambda reduces the variance but increases the bias.
D. As we increase lambda the 2-norm of the weight vector w will also increase.

options.A: In Figure 11 the blue curve with circles corresponds to the training error whereas the black curve with crosses corresponds to the test error.
options.B: According to the model defined for lambda = 0.6952 increasing a players height will increase his average points scored per game.
options.C: There is no optimal way of choosing lambda since increasing lambda reduces the variance but increases the bias.
options.D: As we increase lambda the 2-norm of the weight vector w will also increase.

questions.source_location: exam p.17, Figure 11 (training/test RMSE vs log10(lambda) curves) + fitted model inline

questions.topic: Ridge regression / number of models and generalization estimate
questions.question_text:
We will again consider the ridge regression described in the previous question. Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. Exhaustively evaluating all combinations of features would require the fitting of less models than the proposed ridge-regression procedure.
B. To generate the test curve we need to make predictions from a total of 1060 different models.
C. We can obtain an unbiased estimate of the generalization error of the best performing model from Figure 11.
D. The ridge regression model will be non-linear as the model includes regularization.

options.A: Exhaustively evaluating all combinations of features would require the fitting of less models than the proposed ridge-regression procedure.
options.B: To generate the test curve we need to make predictions from a total of 1060 different models.
options.C: We can obtain an unbiased estimate of the generalization error of the best performing model from Figure 11.
options.D: The ridge regression model will be non-linear as the model includes regularization.

questions.source_location: exam p.18, Question 24 (refers to ridge regression setup from Q23; pure reasoning/computation)

questions.topic: Clustering / choosing the appropriate clustering approach for a scatter
questions.question_text:
Consider the clustering problem given in Figure 12. Figure 12 is a scatter plot in the (x1, x2) plane containing four clusters indicated by black crosses, red circles, magenta plusses, and blue stars. Which clustering approach is most suited for correctly separating the data into the four groups indicated by black crosses, red circles, magenta plusses, and blue asterics?

A. A well-separated clustering approach.
B. A contiguity-based clustering approach.
C. A center-based clustering approach.
D. A conceptual clustering approach.

options.A: A well-separated clustering approach.
options.B: A contiguity-based clustering approach.
options.C: A center-based clustering approach.

questions.source_location: exam p.18, Figure 12 (four-cluster scatter plot in x1-x2 plane)

questions.topic: General ML concepts (multinomial regression, decision trees, local minima, accuracy)
questions.question_text:
Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. Multinomial regression can only handle classification problems where the problem is to classify between two classes.
B. Decision trees return the probability that an observation is in a given class.
C. k-means, Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) are all prone to local minima issues and thus it is recommended to run the procedures using multiple initializations.
D. The accuracy is a good performance measure when facing severe class imbalance issues in a two class classification problem.

options.A: Multinomial regression can only handle classification problems where the problem is to classify between two classes.
options.B: Decision trees return the probability that an observation is in a given class.
options.C: k-means, Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) are all prone to local minima issues and thus it is recommended to run the procedures using multiple initializations.
options.D: The accuracy is a good performance measure when facing severe class imbalance issues in a two class classification problem.

questions.source_location: exam p.18, Question 26 (all text)

questions.topic: Decision tree with norm-based decisions matching decision boundaries
questions.question_text:
We will consider the two class classification problem given in Figure 13 in which the goal is to separate red crosses (i.e., x) from black plusses (i.e., +) based on the decision boundaries in gray and white indicated in the top panel of the figure. Figure 13 shows a scatter in the (x1, x2) plane with a gray region (left half plus a rounded region on the right) classifying red crosses and a white region classifying black plusses. Figure 14 shows a decision tree with four decisions A, B, C, and D (A at the root; if A true classify Class 1 (x); else B; if B true go to C, else go to D; C true -> Class 1 (x), C false -> Class 2 (+); D true -> Class 1 (x), D false -> Class 2 (+)). Which one of the following procedures based on the decision tree given in Figure 14 will perfectly separate the two classes?

A. A = ||x - [0, 0.5]||_inf < 0.5, B = x1 < 0.75, C = ||x - [0.75, 0.5]||_2 < 0.25, D = ||x - [0.75, 0.5]||_1 < 0.25.
B. A = ||x - [0, 0.5]||_1 < 0.5, B = x1 < 0.75, C = ||x - [0.75, 0.5]||_2 < 0.25, D = ||x - [0.75, 0.5]||_inf < 0.25.
C. A = ||x - [0, 0.5]||_inf < 0.5, B = x1 < 0.75, C = ||x - [0.5, 0.75]||_2 < 0.25, D = ||x - [0.5, 0.75]||_1 < 0.25.
D. A = x1 < 0.75, B = ||x - [0, 0.5]||_inf < 0.5, C = ||x - [0.75, 0.5]||_1 < 0.25, D = ||x - [0.75, 0.5]||_2 < 0.25.
questions.source_location: exam p.19, Figure 13 (two-class scatter with gray/white decision regions) + Figure 14 (decision tree structure)
